intro:
The Person of Christ designates the doctrine of how the divine and human natures are united in the one person of Jesus Christ, classically formulated at the Council of Chalcedon (451). The Chalcedonian Definition affirms that Christ is 'one and the same Son, our Lord Jesus Christ' who is 'truly God and truly man,' with two natures 'without confusion, without change, without division, without separation' — existing in one person (hypostasis) and one subsistence. This formulation excluded the Nestorian error (two separate persons) and the Eutychian error (one blended nature) while affirming both the distinctness of natures and the unity of person.

The biblical foundations include the Johannine prologue's identification of the pre-existent Word with the incarnate Jesus (John 1:1-14), Paul's declaration that 'in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' (Col. 2:9), the christological hymn of Philippians 2:6-11 (which describes the Son's self-emptying in taking human form without ceasing to be God), and Hebrews 1:1-4 (the Son as the exact imprint of God's nature and the one through whom God made the universe). The doctrine of the person of Christ is not merely speculative but has soteriological consequences: only one who is fully God can redeem, and only one who is fully human can represent humanity before God. The church's four great councils (Nicaea 325, Constantinople 381, Ephesus 431, Chalcedon 451) progressively defined the grammar of the incarnation precisely because imprecise formulations undermined the coherence of salvation.
